text.title: Prometheus Configuration
text.summary: Scrape intervals, ServiceMonitor and PodMonitor CRDs, recording rules, and alerting philosophy.

rule.statement: Set global scrape interval to 15s. Override to 10s only for critical systems. Use 30–60s for low-priority metrics. Never scrape faster than the metric changes.
rule.rationale: 10s scrape doubles storage and CPU load compared to 15s with minimal benefit for most metrics. Scrape interval below 10s is unsupported in practice — Prometheus needs time to complete each scrape cycle.

example.code:
global: scrape_interval: 15s evaluation_interval: 15s scrape_timeout: 10s
scrape_configs: - job_name: 'kubernetes-pods' kubernetes_sd_configs: - role: pod relabel_configs: - source_labels: [__meta_kubernetes_pod_annotation_prometheus_io_scrape] action: keep regex: 'true'

rule.statement: Use ServiceMonitor for scraping services by label selector. Use PodMonitor only when pods do not have a backing Service. Define PrometheusRule for all recording rules and alerts.
rule.rationale: ServiceMonitor is the standard Prometheus Operator CRD for service-level scraping. It is self-service — teams can add a ServiceMonitor to their namespace without touching prometheus.yaml. PrometheusRule keeps alerts version-controlled alongside the application.

example.code: apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1 kind: ServiceMonitor metadata: name: myapp namespace: monitoring labels: prometheus: main spec: selector: matchLabels: app: myapp namespaceSelector: matchNames: [production] endpoints: - port: metrics interval: 15s path: /metrics

rule.statement: Pre-compute expensive queries used in dashboards as recording rules. Follow the naming format: level:metric:operation (e.g., job:http_requests:rate5m).
rule.rationale: Dashboard queries running on every panel refresh under load multiply Prometheus CPU usage. Recording rules run once per evaluation interval and store the result as a new time series, making dashboards fast regardless of cardinality.

example.code: apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1 kind: PrometheusRule metadata: name: http-recording-rules labels: prometheus: main spec: groups: - name: http.rules interval: 30s rules: - record: job:http_requests:rate5m expr: sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])) by (job) - record: job:http_request_duration_seconds:p99 expr: | histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (job, le) ) - record: job:sli_availability:rate5m expr: | sum(rate(http_requests_total{status!~"5.."}[5m])) by (job) / sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])) by (job)

rule.statement: Alert on symptoms, not causes. Alert on error rate exceeding threshold, not on individual pod failures. Every alert must have a runbook_url annotation.
rule.rationale: Symptom-based alerts (high error rate, high latency) are actionable and describe user impact. Cause-based alerts (pod crashed) create alert fatigue — a pod may crash and restart with no user impact. Runbook URLs eliminate information lookup time during incidents.

rule.statement: Use the for: duration to require alerts to persist before firing. Set at least 2m for critical alerts and 5–15m for warning alerts.
rule.rationale: Transient spikes lasting seconds should not page on-call engineers. A minimum for: duration filters out flapping alerts caused by temporary metric anomalies during deployments or garbage collection.

example.code: - alert: HighErrorRate expr: job:http_requests:error_rate5m > 0.05 for: 5m labels: severity: critical annotations: summary: "High error rate for {{ $labels.job }}" description: "Error rate {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} exceeds 5%" runbook_url: "https://runbooks.example.com/high-error-rate"
